Name: rate_distortion_robustness
Title: Rate-Distortion Robustness in Model Compression: A New Perspective on Information-Theoretic Limits
Short Hypothesis: Can we establish a framework for quantifying the robustness of model compression schemes under varying distortion measures, linking them to theoretical rate-distortion bounds and operational limits? This question is critical as existing works primarily focus on static performance metrics without exploring the dynamic robustness of compression schemes in response to task or distributional shifts.
Related Work: Existing literature primarily addresses static rate-distortion trade-offs without incorporating robustness to perturbations or shifts in data distributions. While recent works like 'Rate Distortion via Constrained Estimated Mutual Information Minimization' and 'Rate-Distortion-Classification Model In Lossy Image Compression' focus on theoretical bounds, they do not consider how these bounds can adapt to varying operational conditions. My proposal distinguishes itself by integrating dynamic robustness into the rate-distortion framework, thereby enhancing the applicability of theoretical findings in real-world scenarios.
Abstract: This research proposes a novel framework for understanding the robustness of model compression schemes through the lens of rate-distortion theory. We aim to quantify how different compression strategies maintain predictive performance under varying distortion measures, particularly in the presence of distributional shifts. By leveraging mutual information and description length metrics, we establish a robust rate-distortion frontier that reflects not only static efficiency but also adaptability to task-specific requirements. This framework will allow us to derive operational bounds on bits-per-weight necessary to preserve predictive sufficiency under dynamic conditions. Through theoretical derivations and empirical validation, we will demonstrate how our approach can inform the design of compression methods that are resilient to changes in input data characteristics, ultimately contributing to the development of more robust machine learning systems.
Experiments: 1. Test various model compression techniques (e.g., pruning, quantization) across benchmark datasets with controlled distributional shifts to observe predictive performance changes. 2. Quantify the robustness of these techniques using a new metric that combines traditional distortion measures with task-specific metrics (e.g., classification accuracy). 3. Derive theoretical bounds and compare them with empirical results to validate the proposed framework. Evaluation metrics will include compression ratio, predictive accuracy, and robustness scores based on distributional shifts.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The framework may introduce complexity in computation, especially when defining new robustness metrics. 2. Empirical validation may require extensive experimentation across diverse datasets, which can be resource-intensive. 3. The theoretical bounds may not hold under all conditions, necessitating careful framing of assumptions and limitations.
